For every final state ω ∈ Ω the change relative to the status-quo is

The individual evaluates only the change, and with the linear
value-function V the (deterministic) utility of a change equals the
change itself:

  V(Δ) = Δ.                                          (2)

Hence an action a is assessed by the expected change

  U(a) = E [ V(Δ(a)) ] = E [ Δ(a) ].                 (3)

Actions and their distributions over the random change Δ are

Action 𝒜₃ :     P(Δ = 0) = 0.75, P(Δ = +2) = 0.25.   (4)

The final (post-decision) state is always

  ω = s₀ + Δ.                                       (5)

• 𝒜₃

Therefore

• 𝒜₁ : ω = –3 + 0.5 = –2.5 with probability 1

Although the lotteries differ in risk, they are identical with respect

The optimisation problem is

 𝒶* ∈ arg supₐ E[V(Δ(a))] = arg supₐ E[Δ(a)].       (7)

Because every admissible action yields the same expected value 0.5,
the set of maximisers is the whole action set:

 𝒜* = {𝒜₁, 𝒜₂, 𝒜₃}.                                  (8)

In words: under a linear reference-point utility the decision maker is
indifferent among the three actions; each increases the expected
change relative to the status-quo by +0.5.  A preference would emerge
only if the value function were non-linear (risk aversion/seeking) or

   EU(𝒜₁) = E[ V(Δ) | 𝒜₁ ]  
           = 1·0.5 = 0.5                                  (4a)

   EU(𝒜₂) = 0.50·0 + 0.50·1 = 0.50                       (4b)

   EU(𝒜₃) = 0.75·0 + 0.25·2 = 0.50                       (4c)

   Hence EU(𝒜₁) = EU(𝒜₂) = EU(𝒜₃) = 0.5.  

4. Variances (only for information)

   Var(𝒜₁) = 0                         (no risk)  
   Var(𝒜₂) = 0.25                      (moderate risk)  
   Var(𝒜₃) = 0.75                      (highest risk)

5. Optimal action set  

We solve  

        𝒶* = arg sup_{𝒶∈{𝒜₁,𝒜₂,𝒜₃}} E[V(Δ(𝒶))].         (5)

Because all three actions yield the same expected value 0.5, every action is an optimizer:

        𝒶* = {𝒜₁, 𝒜₂, 𝒜₃}.                               (6)

If a single representative is required, one may pick 𝒜₁ because it attains the optimum with zero variance, but under strict risk–neutral expected-value maximisation the decision maker is indifferent among the three.

1.   Problem data

     a* = arg supₐ  E[ V(Δ(a)) ] , with V(Δ) = U(Δ) = Δ.  

4.   Expected utilities  

     E[V(Δ) | A₁] = 1·0.5 = 0.5

     E[V(Δ)|A₁] = E[V(Δ)|A₂] = E[V(Δ)|A₃] = 0.5  
     ⇒ sup value = 0.5.  

6.   Solution set  

     Any of the three actions yields the maximal expected value.  
     Hence  
                a* ∈ {A₁ , A₂ , A₃}.

        𝒜₃ :   P(Δ = 0)             = 0.75

   Because V(Δ) = Δ, the expected utility of an action is simply its

              𝔼[V(Δ)] = 𝔼[Δ] = Σ Δ · P(Δ).

         𝔼[Δ | 𝒜₁] = (+0.5) · 1 = 0.5 .

   attains it:

         𝒜*  =  {𝒜₁, 𝒜₂, 𝒜₃}.                          □

(The tie would be broken only by introducing some risk attitude or an

There is no unique optimum when the decision maker is risk-neutral (linear V).  
If one were to introduce risk attitudes, ties would be broken (e.g. a risk-averse agent would prefer the sure gain 𝒜₁, while a risk-seeker might prefer 𝒜₃), but under the stated criterion of expected value alone the decision maker is indifferent among the three actions.

Step 1 – Set-up  
Reference (status-quo) state   s₀ = –2  
For every outcome ωᵢ we work with the change relative to s₀  

 Δ(ωᵢ) = ωᵢ – s₀.  

The decision maker’s “value function’’ is linear:   V(Δ) = Δ.  
Hence maximising the expected value is the same as maximising the expected change E[Δ].

Step 2 – Distributions of the change Δ produced by each action  

A₁ : P(Δ = +0.5) = 1   (deterministic)  
A₂ : P(Δ = 0) = 0.5, P(Δ = +1) = 0.5  
A₃ : P(Δ = 0) = 0.75, P(Δ = +2) = 0.25  

Step 3 – Expected change ( = expected value)  

 E[Δ│A₁] = (+0.5) · 1           = 0.5  

 E[Δ│A₂] = (0)·0.5 + (+1)·0.5  = 0.5  

 E[Δ│A₃] = (0)·0.75 + (+2)·0.25 = 0.5  

Step 4 – Maximisation  

All three actions yield exactly the same expected value

 E[V(Δ)]    = 0.5.

Therefore the supremum of expected value is 0.5 and every one of the three actions attains it.

 𝒶* ∈ {𝒜₁, 𝒜₂, 𝒜₃}.  

(If a unique choice is desired, any tie-breaking rule can be invoked; a natural one for a risk-neutral but variance-averse decision maker would pick 𝒜₁ because it is risk-free.)

Step 5 – Final states (for completeness)  

A₁ : final state –1.5 (certain)  
A₂ : final state –2 (p = 0.5), –1 (p = 0.5)  
A₃ : final state –2 (p = 0.75),  0 (p = 0.25)  

All have the same expected final state –1.5, differing only in risk.

 • 𝒜₂ : E[V] = 0.5·0 + 0.5·1 = 0.5

 The objective is  
  𝒶* = arg supₐ 𝔼[V(Δ(𝒶))].

E[V(∆(𝒜₁))] = E[V(∆(𝒜₂))] = E[V(∆(𝒜₃))] = +0.5.  
All three actions yield the same maximal expected reference-point value.



STEP 3 Optimal action set

𝒜* = arg sup_𝒜 E[V(∆(𝒜))] = { 𝒜₁ , 𝒜₂ , 𝒜₃ }.



(The decision maker is indifferent among the three because the valuation function is linear in ∆; the risk characteristics differ but do not affect the expected value when V = ∆.)

Hence any of the three actions attains the supremum expected value of 0.5.

   • 𝒜₃ : s_f = –2 (with p = 0.75) or  0 (with p = 0.25)

E[V(Δ)|𝒜₃] = 0·0.75 + 2·0.25 = +0.5

The game is defined only for final states ω ∈ Ω.

constraint

and must be discarded.

𝒜₂ and 𝒜₃ satisfy the constraint.

For the admissible actions we have

Therefore  

 sup_{𝒶} E[V(Δ(𝒶))] = 0.5

and the set of maximisers is  

 𝒶* = { 𝒜₂ , 𝒜₃ }.

Any action that leaves the final state in Ω and yields an expected gain

has to be excluded because its final state is not contained in Ω.

                     = 0.5

          E[V(Δ│𝒜₃)] = 0·¾ + 2·¼
                     = 0.5

     Hence  

          supₐ  E[V(Δ(a))] = 0.5 .

3.   Optimal action set  

     All three actions reach that supremum; therefore

          𝒜*  =  {𝒜₁, 𝒜₂, 𝒜₃}.  

     Under the stipulated risk-neutral criterion the decision maker is
     indifferent among the three lotteries.

4.   (Extra) Higher-order risk considerations  

     Variances of the three lotteries are  

          Var(Δ│𝒜₁) = 0  
          Var(Δ│𝒜₂) = 0.25  
          Var(Δ│𝒜₃) = 0.75 .

     A risk-averse agent would choose 𝒜₁ (lowest variance); a risk-seeking
     agent might prefer 𝒜₃ (greater upside), but with the linear value
     function stated in the problem all actions are equally optimal.

MATHEMATICAL ANALYSIS  

1.  Reference point and value function  
   Initial state                    : s₀ = −2  
   Reference point                  : R = 0 (irrelevant here because V(.) is linear in Δ)  
   State-change (outcome) variable  : Δ = ω − s₀  
   Value function                   : V(Δ) = Δ  

2.  Probability distributions of Δ produced by the three feasible actions  

   𝒜₁ : P(Δ = +0.5) = 1.0  
   𝒜₂ : P(Δ = 0)    = 0.5, P(Δ = +1) = 0.5  
   𝒜₃ : P(Δ = 0)    = 0.75, P(Δ = +2) = 0.25  

3.  Expected value of each action (because V is linear, E[V(Δ)] = E[Δ])  

   E[V(Δ)|𝒜₁] = (0.5)·1 = 0.5  

   E[V(Δ)|𝒜₂] = (0)·0.5 + (1)·0.5 = 0.50  

   E[V(Δ)|𝒜₃] = (0)·0.75 + (2)·0.25 = 0.50  

4.  Maximisation  

   supₐ E[V(Δ(𝒶))] = 0.5  

   The maximising set (arg sup) is  
   𝒶* = {𝒜₁, 𝒜₂, 𝒜₃}.  

All three actions deliver the same maximal expected value of 0.5, hence the decision-maker is indifferent among them under the stated (risk-neutral) value function.

Step 1 – definition of the objective  

For every action 𝒶 the decision maker evaluates only the change (the “gain/loss’’) with respect to the present state  

  Δ = final state – s₀.  

Because the value function is linear, V(Δ)=Δ, the objective that has to be maximised is the expected change  

  𝔼[V(Δ(𝒶))] = 𝔼[Δ(𝒶)] = Σ_d P_𝒶(Δ=d)·d.

Step 2 – expected value of each admissible action  

Action 𝒜₁  
 Δ=+0.5 with probability 1  
 𝔼[Δ|𝒜₁]=1·0.5 = 0.5.

Action 𝒜₂  
 Δ=0 with probability 0.5, Δ=+1 with probability 0.5  
 𝔼[Δ|𝒜₂]=0.5·0 + 0.5·1 = 0.5.

Action 𝒜₃  
 Δ=0 with probability 0.75, Δ=+2 with probability 0.25  
 𝔼[Δ|𝒜₃]=0.75·0 + 0.25·2 = 0.5.

Step 3 – comparison  

𝔼[Δ|𝒜₁]=𝔼[Δ|𝒜₂]=𝔼[Δ|𝒜₃]=0.5.  
Hence the maximal expected value is 0.5 and all three actions attain it.

Step 4 – solution set  

𝒜* = {𝒜₁, 𝒜₂, 𝒜₃}.  
The decision maker is indifferent among the three actions, any of them is optimal.

1.  Problem data  

   • Reference- (initial) state :     s₀ = –2  
   • Possible absolute states    :     Ω = { –2, –1, 0, +1, +2 }  
   • Gain / loss (relative change) : Δ(ω) = ω – s₀  
   • Value function                     : V(Δ) = Δ (i.e. linear in the change)  

2.  Change distributions attached to the three feasible actions  

           𝒜₁ : P(Δ = +0.5) = 1  
           𝒜₂ : P(Δ = 0) = 0.5 , P(Δ = +1) = 0.5  
           𝒜₃ : P(Δ = 0) = 0.75, P(Δ = +2) = 0.25  

   (Final state produced by an action is, by the given constraint,  
    s_f = s₀ + Δ.  This is listed only to keep track; it plays no role in
    the expected-value maximisation carried out below.)

3.  Objective  

        Find 𝒶* = arg supₐ 𝔼[V(Δ(a))]  
        Because V is linear (V(Δ)=Δ), this is equivalent to maximising the
        expected change itself:  𝔼[V(Δ)] = 𝔼[Δ].

4.  Expected values for each action  

                                            ⎧ +0.5 w.p. 1                ⎫  
    𝔼[Δ | 𝒜₁] = (+0.5)(1) = 0.5               ⎪                             ⎪  
                                            ⎩                             ⎭  

                                   ⎧ 0 w.p. 0.5 , +1 w.p. 0.5 ⎫  
    𝔼[Δ | 𝒜₂] = 0·0.5 + 1·0.5 = 0.5           ⎩                      ⎭  

                              ⎧ 0 w.p. 0.75 , +2 w.p. 0.25 ⎫  
    𝔼[Δ | 𝒜₃] = 0·0.75 + 2·0.25 = 0.50        ⎩                    ⎭  

   All three actions deliver exactly the same expected gain:

                        𝔼[V(Δ(𝒜₁))] = 𝔼[V(Δ(𝒜₂))] = 𝔼[V(Δ(𝒜₃))] = 0.5.

5.  Maximisation and solution set  

   Because the supremum of the expected value is 0.5 and every action
   attains it, there is no unique maximiser.  The set of optimal actions is

               𝒜*  = { 𝒜₁ , 𝒜₂ , 𝒜₃ }.

   If an additional requirement were introduced (e.g. “the final state must
   belong to Ω”), action 𝒜₁ would be eliminated (it ends at –1.5), leaving
   𝒜₂ and 𝒜₃ as the remaining maximisers; but under the formulation given
   in the statement all three actions are equally optimal.

MATHEMATICAL ANALYSIS  

1. Objects that are given  
   Reference point            s₀ = –2  
   State space                Ω = { –2, –1, 0, +1, +2 }  
   Gain/Loss mapping          Δ(ω) = ω – s₀  
   Value (utility) function   V(Δ) = Δ           (identity → risk–neutral)  

2. Admissible actions (lotteries over Δ)  

   𝒜₁ : P(Δ = +0.5)      = 1  
   𝒜₂ : P(Δ = 0)         = 0.5 ,   P(Δ = +1) = 0.5  
   𝒜₃ : P(Δ = 0)         = 0.75,   P(Δ = +2) = 0.25  

   By the constraint “final_state = s₀ + Δ”, the corresponding terminal
   states are

              𝒜₁ : –2 + 0.5  = –1.5  
              𝒜₂ : –2 + 0    = –2  (prob. 0.5)
                    –2 + 1    = –1  (prob. 0.5)
              𝒜₃ : –2 + 0    = –2  (prob. 0.75)
                    –2 + 2    =  0  (prob. 0.25)

   (If the model requires the terminal state to lie in Ω, then 𝒜₁ would be
   infeasible; the calculations below remain valid otherwise.)

3. Expected value of every action  
   Because V(Δ) = Δ, the expected utility is simply the expected Δ.

          E[V|𝒜₁] = 1·0.5            = 0.5
          E[V|𝒜₂] = 0.5·0 + 0.5·1     = 0.5
          E[V|𝒜₃] = 0.75·0 + 0.25·2   = 0.50

4. Optimal action(s)  
   E[V] is identical (= 0.5) for all three lotteries, hence

          𝒜* = arg sup E[V(Δ)] = {𝒜₁, 𝒜₂, 𝒜₃}.   

   • If terminal states must belong to Ω, rule out 𝒜₁ and we obtain  
     𝒜* = {𝒜₂, 𝒜₃}.  

5. Risk characteristics (optional, but often reported)  
   Var(Δ|𝒜₁) = 0  (risk-free)  
   Var(Δ|𝒜₂) = 0.25  
   Var(Δ|𝒜₃) = 0.75  

   – A risk-averse decision maker would prefer 𝒜₁ ≻ 𝒜₂ ≻ 𝒜₃.  
   – A risk-seeker would prefer 𝒜₃ ≻ 𝒜₂ ≻ 𝒜₁.  
   – A risk-neutral (as in the present specification) is indifferent.

6. Conclusion  
   With the specified linear value function the expected utility of every
   admissible action equals 0.5.  Consequently every action that satisfies
   the state-space constraint is optimal:

        𝒜*  = 𝒜₁ = 𝒜₂ = 𝒜₃   (if –1.5 ∈ Ω)  
        𝒜*  = {𝒜₂, 𝒜₃}      (if final state must be in Ω).

1.  Reference point and value-function  
   Reference (status-quo) state s₀ = –2.  
   For every outcome ω the decision maker evaluates only the
   change (gain/loss)

  Δ(ω) = ω – s₀ ,  V(Δ) = Δ.                             (1)

   Hence the expected subjective value of an action 𝒶 that induces the
   distribution P𝒶 over the changes {Δₖ} is  

  E[V(Δ(𝒶))] = Σₖ P𝒶(Δₖ) · Δₖ.                          (2)


2.  Feasible changes supplied by the three actions  

   𝒜₁ : P(Δ = +0.5) = 1.0                               (3a)  
   𝒜₂ : P(Δ = 0)   = 0.5, P(Δ = +1) = 0.5               (3b)  
   𝒜₃ : P(Δ = 0)   = 0.75, P(Δ = +2) = 0.25             (3c)

   The resulting (absolute) final states are obtained from

  final state = s₀ + Δ.                                 (4)

   • 𝒜₁ → final state = –2 + 0.5 = –1.5  
   • 𝒜₂ → final states = –2 (with p = 0.5) or –1 (with p = 0.5)  
   • 𝒜₃ → final states = –2 (with p = 0.75) or  0 (with p = 0.25)

   Remark: if one additionally imposes the feasibility requirement
   “final state ∈ Ω = {–2, –1, 0, +1, +2}”, then 𝒜₁ would be ruled
   out because –1.5 ∉ Ω.  The calculations below are valid with or
   without that feasibility restriction; at the end we point out the
   consequences.


3.  Expected subjective value of every action  

   • 𝒜₁  

  E[V(Δ(𝒜₁))] = 1.0 · (+0.5) = +0.5.                    (5a)

   • 𝒜₂  

  E[V(Δ(𝒜₂))] = 0.5·0 + 0.5·(+1) = +0.5.               (5b)

   • 𝒜₃  

  E[V(Δ(𝒜₃))] = 0.75·0 + 0.25·(+2) = +0.5.             (5c)


4.  Maximisation  

   All three expectations are identical:

  E[V(Δ(𝒜₁))] = E[V(Δ(𝒜₂))] = E[V(Δ(𝒜₃))] = 0.5.       (6)

   Therefore the supremum of the expected value is 0.5, and every
   action that attains this level is optimal.

   • Without any further feasibility restriction:

  𝒶* ∈ {𝒜₁, 𝒜₂, 𝒜₃}.                                   (7)

   • If we insist that the final state must lie inside Ω, then 𝒜₁ is
     infeasible, leaving

  𝒶* ∈ {𝒜₂, 𝒜₃}.                                       (8)

   (Both still deliver the maximal expected subjective value 0.5; they
   differ only in variance—𝒜₂ is less risky.)


5.  Solution  

Maximum attainable expected value: supₐ E[V(Δ(𝒶))] = 0.5.  
Argmax (set of optimal actions):  

 • {𝒜₁, 𝒜₂, 𝒜₃} if final-state feasibility is not enforced;  
 • {𝒜₂, 𝒜₃}  if final-state ∈ Ω is required.

Let  

A = {𝒜₁, 𝒜₂, 𝒜₃},  V(Δ) = Δ (risk–neutral, linear utility),  s₀ = –2.  

For every action 𝒜 ∈ A we must compute  

𝔼[V(Δ(𝒜))] = Σ P(Δ = d | 𝒜) · V(d) = Σ P(Δ = d | 𝒜) · d.  

Action 𝒜₁  
 P(Δ = +0.5) = 1  

 𝔼[V(Δ(𝒜₁))] = 1 · 0.5 = 0.5.  

Action 𝒜₂  
 P(Δ = 0)   = 0.5  
 P(Δ = +1)  = 0.5  

 𝔼[V(Δ(𝒜₂))] = 0.5·0 + 0.5·1 = 0.5.  

Action 𝒜₃  
 P(Δ = 0)  = 0.75  
 P(Δ = +2) = 0.25  

 𝔼[V(Δ(𝒜₃))] = 0.75·0 + 0.25·2 = 0.5.  

Thus  

𝔼[V(Δ(𝒜₁))] = 𝔼[V(Δ(𝒜₂))] = 𝔼[V(Δ(𝒜₃))] = 0.5.  

Because the expected value of the gain Δ is identical (0.5) for all three actions, they are all optimal with respect to the given evaluation criterion:

𝒶* = arg sup𝒜 𝔼[V(Δ(𝒜))] = {𝒜₁, 𝒜₂, 𝒜₃}.

MATHEMATICAL ANALYSIS  

1. Reference point  
   s₀ = –2  

2. Evaluation rule  
   For any realised state ωᵢ ∈ Ω  
        Δ(ωᵢ) = ωᵢ – s₀        (reference–point gain / loss)  
        V(Δ)   = Δ              (value function is linear → risk–neutral)  

3. Actions and their ∆–distributions  

   𝒜₁ :  P(Δ = +0.5)               = 1.0  

   𝒜₂ :  P(Δ = 0)                  = 0.5  
         P(Δ = +1)                 = 0.5  

   𝒜₃ :  P(Δ = 0)                  = 0.75  
         P(Δ = +2)                 = 0.25  

4. Expected value of each action  

   𝔼[V(Δ)|𝒜₁] = ( +0.5 ) (1.0)                  = 0.50  

   𝔼[V(Δ)|𝒜₂] = ( 0 ) (0.5) + ( +1 ) (0.5)      = 0.50  

   𝔼[V(Δ)|𝒜₃] = ( 0 ) (0.75) + ( +2 ) (0.25)    = 0.50  

5. Optimal action(s)  

   All three actions yield the same maximal expected value  
        supₐ 𝔼[V(Δ(𝒶))] = 0.50  

   Hence the set of maximisers is  
        𝒶* = { 𝒜₁ , 𝒜₂ , 𝒜₃ }  

6. Final-state expectations (sanity check)  

   Expected final state = s₀ + 𝔼[Δ] = –2 + 0.5 = –1.5 under every action, confirming the tie.

Conclusion: with a linear value function the decision maker is indifferent; any of 𝒜₁, 𝒜₂ or 𝒜₃ is optimal.

Conclusion  
Any of the three actions maximises the expected reference-point value; the solution set is 𝒜*.

ordinary mathematical expectation of the random variable Δ that action 𝒶
generates:

where {pᵢ(𝒶)} are the probabilities attached to the possible increments

Because the reference point is s₀ = –2, the “final state’’ that an
action produces is

          s_f  =  s₀ + Δ = –2 + Δ.                       (3)

The optimisation problem that has to be solved is therefore

          𝒶*  =  arg sup 𝒶 E[𝒶].                       (4)

  Δ takes the single value +0.5 with probability 1:
          E[𝒜₁]  =  1·(+0.5)  =  0.5.                   (5)

Action 𝒜₂  
  Δ = 0  with probability 0.5  
  Δ = +1 with probability 0.5
          E[𝒜₂]  =  0.5·0  +  0.5·(+1)  =  0.5.         (6)

Action 𝒜₃

        𝒶* = arg sup 𝔼[V(Δ(𝒶))] .                     (4)  

   Because no other “value function” V is specified, we take V = U of (2); hence

        𝒶* = arg sup 𝔼[Δ(𝒶)].                         (5)  

4.  Expected value (utility) of each action  

   – Action 𝒜₁  

        𝔼[Δ|𝒜₁] = (1)·(+0.5) = 0.5.                  (6)  

   – Action 𝒜₂  

        𝔼[Δ|𝒜₂] = (0.5)·0 + (0.5)·(+1) = 0.5.         (7)  

   – Action 𝒜₃  

        𝔼[Δ|𝒜₃] = (0.75)·0 + (0.25)·(+2) = 0.5.       (8)  

5.  Maximisation and solution set  

        supₐ 𝔼[Δ(𝒶)] = 0.5,                            (9)  

   and all three admissible actions attain this supremum:

        𝔼[Δ|𝒜₁] = 𝔼[Δ|𝒜₂] = 𝔼[Δ|𝒜₃] = 0.5.            (10)  

   Therefore

        𝒜* ∈ {𝒜₁, 𝒜₂, 𝒜₃}.                              (11)  

   The decision maker is indifferent among the three because, under a linear value function, they all provide the same expected gain of +0.5 relative to the reference point.

6.  (Remark)  If the value function were non-linear (e.g. concave under risk-aversion or S-shaped as in prospect theory), the ranking could change because the actions have different dispersions around their means.  With U(Δ)=Δ, however, only the mean matters and the three actions are equivalent.

All three actions produce the same expected change (and therefore the same expected utility)

         E[Δ|𝒜₁] = E[Δ|𝒜₂] = E[Δ|𝒜₃] = 0.5.

6. Solution  

Because the expected value is identical for all admissible actions, every action is an optimiser:

         𝒶* ∈ {𝒜₁, 𝒜₂, 𝒜₃}.

If a secondary tie–breaking rule were introduced (for example, risk aversion so that lower variance is preferred), 𝒜₁ would be picked because Var[Δ|𝒜₁] = 0 < Var[Δ|𝒜₂]=0.25 < Var[Δ|𝒜₃]=0.75.  
Without any such rule, the decision maker is simply indifferent amongst the three actions.
